title: Differentiation Techniques
blurb: The three rules that unlock every function you can write: product, quotient, and — the big one — the chain rule, plus second derivatives and how to stack the rules without dropping anything.
buildsOn: The power rule and the four library derivatives from Unit 2.

lessons.title: The Product Rule
lessons.concreteComparison: A rectangle's area grows two ways at once: stretch the width while the height stands, and stretch the height while the width stands. Total growth = both contributions. The product rule is exactly that accounting: each factor takes a turn changing while the other holds still.
lessons.objective: Differentiate products with (fg)′ = f′g + fg′, and see why the naive f′g′ fails.
concept: **Product rule:** $(fg)' = f'g + fg'$. Each factor takes one turn being differentiated while the other rides along unchanged, and the turns ADD. It is emphatically NOT $f'g'$ — differentiation doesn't distribute over multiplication.

The rectangle picture explains the formula: a rectangle with sides $f$ and $g$ gains area $f' g$ (width stretching against standing height) plus $f g'$ (height stretching against standing width). Two contributions, added — the tiny corner sliver $f'g'$ vanishes in the limit.

Cheap self-test: $x \cdot x = x^2$ should give $2x$. Product rule: $1 \cdot x + x \cdot 1 = 2x$ ✓. Naive $f'g'$: $1 \cdot 1 = 1$ ✗. When you can multiply out first (two polynomials), do it as a cross-check — both roads must land on the same derivative.
lessons.keyIdea: (fg)′ = f′g + fg′ — each factor takes a turn changing while the other stands still, and the turns add; f′g′ is wrong, always.

facts.title: Product rule
facts.explanation: Turn-taking: differentiate one factor per term, add the terms.

facts.title: The rectangle reason
facts.latex: \Delta(fg) \approx f' g \, \Delta x + f g' \, \Delta x
facts.explanation: Two slivers of area growth; the corner crumb vanishes in the limit.

facts.title: The self-test
facts.explanation: The naive f′g′ = 1 fails the simplest case there is.

workedExamples.statement: Differentiate $y = (x^2 + 3)(x^3 - 1)$ two ways: product rule, then expand-first. Confirm they agree.
workedExamples.solution: Product rule: $2x(x^3 - 1) + (x^2 + 3)(3x^2) = 2x^4 - 2x + 3x^4 + 9x^2 = 5x^4 + 9x^2 - 2x$. Expand first: $y = x^5 + 3x^3 - x^2 - 3$, so $y' = 5x^4 + 9x^2 - 2x$. Same answer, two roads — the rule earns its keep when expanding ISN'T possible.
No way to 'expand' a polynomial times a wave — the rule is mandatory. Turns: $(x^3)' \cos x + x^3 (\cos x)' = 3x^2 \cos x - x^3 \sin x$. Watch the minus arrive with cosine's derivative.

commonMistakes.text: Differentiating both factors at once: (fg)′ = f′g′.
commonMistakes.correction: Test it on $x \cdot x$: naive gives $1$, but $(x^2)' = 2x$. Differentiation takes TURNS — one factor changes per term, the other stands still, and the terms add: $f'g + fg'$.

commonMistakes.text: Leaving the answer unsimplified and factoring wrong later.
commonMistakes.correction: After the rule, collect like terms while the structure is fresh: $2x(x^3-1) + 3x^2(x^2+3)$ should become $5x^4 + 9x^2 - 2x$ on the spot. Downstream work (tangents, zeros) needs the collected form.

tryIt.statement: Differentiate $y = (x^2 + 1)(x^2 - 5x)$ with the product rule, then verify by expanding first.
Differentiate $y = x e^x$ and evaluate the slope at $x = 0$.
tryIt.solution: Turns: $1 \cdot e^x + x \cdot e^x = e^x(1 + x)$. At 0: $e^0(1 + 0) = 1$.

steps.title: Sums split. Products don't.
steps.body: Unit 2 left a hole: $(f + g)' = f' + g'$ splits cleanly, so surely $(fg)' = f'g'$? Test it on the easiest product alive: $x \cdot x = x^2$. True derivative: $2x$. Naive guess: $1 \cdot 1 = 1$. Dead on arrival. Products need their own rule — and it's a good one.

steps.title: Take turns
steps.body: **$(fg)' = f'g + fg'$.** Read it as turn-taking: first term, $f$ changes while $g$ stands still; second term, $g$ changes while $f$ stands. Picture a rectangle with sides $f$ and $g$: nudge $x$ and the area gains a sliver along one edge ($f'g$) plus a sliver along the other ($fg'$). The corner crumb where both grow at once? Doubly tiny — the limit erases it.

steps.title: Sanity first
steps.prompt: Run the product rule on $x \cdot x$. What comes out?
steps.explanation: Each factor takes a turn: $1 \cdot x + x \cdot 1 = 2x$, exactly the power rule's answer. One tiny example, and it separates the right rule from the tempting wrong one forever.

steps.title: When expanding is impossible
steps.body: Two polynomials you could always multiply out. But $x^3 \cos x$? $x e^x$? There's no 'expanded form' of a polynomial times a wave — the product rule is the ONLY road. This is the pattern for the whole unit: the rules exist for the functions that algebra alone can't flatten.

steps.title: Your turn to take turns
steps.explanation: Turns: $(x^2)'\sin x + x^2(\sin x)' = 2x\sin x + x^2\cos x$. Option B is the naive $f'g'$ trap; option C puts the minus where no minus belongs (that's cosine's move, not sine's).

steps.title: Say it as you write it
steps.body: Narrate the rule under your breath: 'derivative of the FIRST times the second, PLUS the first times derivative of the SECOND.' The chant keeps the turns straight when the factors get ugly — and it scales: with practice, three-factor products just take three turns.

steps.title: What sticks
points: (fg)′ = f′g + fg′ — turn-taking, terms added; f′g′ fails even on x·x.
The rectangle picture: two slivers of growth, one per factor; the corner crumb vanishes.
When both factors are polynomials, expand-first is a free cross-check.

lessons.title: The Quotient Rule
lessons.concreteComparison: A fraction is a tug-of-war: the top pulling the value up, the bottom dragging it down. The quotient rule scores that tug-of-war — top's contribution MINUS bottom's contribution — and because it's a competition, the order of the subtraction is everything.
lessons.objective: Differentiate quotients with (f/g)′ = (f′g − fg′)/g², keeping the order straight, and simplify the results.
concept: **Quotient rule:** $\left(\frac{f}{g}\right)' = \frac{f'g - fg'}{g^2}$. Top-prime-first, MINUS, all over the bottom squared. Unlike the product rule's symmetric $+$, the minus makes order fatal: swap the terms and every sign in your answer flips.

The memory chant: 'low d-high minus high d-low, over low-low.' Bottom times derivative-of-top, minus top times derivative-of-bottom, over bottom squared. Silly, and twenty times harder to forget than the formula.

Simplify with intent: expand the numerator and collect — the $g^2$ downstairs usually stays factored. For $\frac{3x - 2}{x + 4}$: numerator $3(x + 4) - (3x - 2)(1) = 14$, so the derivative is $\frac{14}{(x+4)^2}$ — positive everywhere it's defined, so this curve NEVER falls. A one-line derivative can describe an entire graph.
lessons.keyIdea: (f/g)′ = (f′g − fg′)/g² — low d-high minus high d-low over low-low; the minus makes order everything.

facts.title: Quotient rule
facts.explanation: Top's turn first, then MINUS the bottom's turn, over g².

facts.title: The chant
facts.explanation: Order-proof mnemonic; the minus is the dangerous part.

facts.title: Sign bonus
facts.explanation: The squared bottom never flips the sign — read rise/fall from the top alone.

workedExamples.statement: Differentiate $y = \dfrac{3x - 2}{x + 4}$ and explain what the result says about the graph.
workedExamples.solution: Low d-high minus high d-low: $\frac{(x + 4)(3) - (3x - 2)(1)}{(x + 4)^2} = \frac{3x + 12 - 3x + 2}{(x+4)^2} = \frac{14}{(x + 4)^2}$. Numerator constant and positive, denominator a square: $y' > 0$ everywhere it exists — the curve climbs on BOTH sides of its asymptote $x = -4$, without exception.

workedExamples.statement: Differentiate $y = \dfrac{x^2}{x - 1}$ and find where its tangent is horizontal.
workedExamples.solution: $\frac{2x(x - 1) - x^2(1)}{(x - 1)^2} = \frac{x^2 - 2x}{(x - 1)^2} = \frac{x(x - 2)}{(x-1)^2}$. Horizontal tangents where the NUMERATOR dies (the square below can't): $x = 0$ and $x = 2$ — a local peak and valley straddling the asymptote at $x = 1$.

commonMistakes.text: Writing the numerator backwards: fg′ − f′g.
commonMistakes.correction: The minus makes order LETHAL: backwards gives the exact negative of the right answer — rising graphs report as falling. Chant it: LOW d-high (first), minus HIGH d-low (second). When in doubt, test on $\frac{x^2}{x} = x$, whose derivative must be 1.

commonMistakes.text: Reaching for the quotient rule when the denominator is a constant — or a lone power.
commonMistakes.correction: $\frac{x^3 - 5x}{7}$ is just $\frac{1}{7}(x^3 - 5x)$: constant-multiple rule, ten seconds. $\frac{4}{x^2}$ is $4x^{-2}$: power rule. Save the heavy machinery for genuinely variable-over-variable fractions.

tryIt.statement: Differentiate $y = \dfrac{2x + 1}{x^2 + 1}$ and evaluate the slope at $x = 0$.
Differentiate $y = \dfrac{x}{x + 3}$ and show the graph is increasing everywhere on its domain.
tryIt.solution: $\frac{1 \cdot (x + 3) - x \cdot 1}{(x + 3)^2} = \frac{3}{(x + 3)^2}$. Positive constant over a square: $y' > 0$ at every $x \neq -3$ — always climbing.

steps.title: The tug-of-war formula
steps.body: Products take turns; quotients COMPETE. **$\left(\frac{f}{g}\right)' = \frac{f'g - fg'}{g^2}$**: the top's turn enters positive (top growing pulls the fraction up), the bottom's turn enters NEGATIVE (bottom growing drags it down), and the whole score sits over $g^2$. That minus sign is the entire personality of this rule.

steps.title: Low d-high minus high d-low
steps.body: Generations of students keep the order straight with one chant: '**low d-high minus high d-low, over low-low.**' Low = bottom, high = top, d = derivative. Try it on $\frac{x^2}{x} = x$: low d-high $= x \cdot 2x = 2x^2$, high d-low $= x^2 \cdot 1$, difference $x^2$, over $x^2$: derivative 1. ✓ — exactly $(x)'$. The chant survives exams; bare formulas don't.

steps.title: Order check
steps.prompt: Which is the correct numerator for $\left(\dfrac{f}{g}\right)'$?
steps.explanation: Top's derivative leads, bottom's turn is subtracted: $f'g - fg'$. Option B is the sign-flipped disaster; option C is the product rule wandering into the wrong lesson; option D is always wrong everywhere.

steps.title: A derivative that reads the graph
The bottom can't flip signs
steps.body: The denominator $g^2$ is a SQUARE — positive wherever it's defined. So the SIGN of a quotient-rule derivative lives entirely in the numerator. Want to know where $\frac{x^2}{x-1}$ rises and falls? Solve $x^2 - 2x > 0$ and ignore the bottom completely. Half the analysis, free.

steps.title: Spot the overkill
steps.prompt: Which derivative does NOT need the quotient rule?
steps.explanation: A constant bottom is just a coefficient: $\frac{1}{5}(x^4 - 2x)$, derivative $\frac{4x^3 - 2}{5}$, done. The rule is for variable-over-variable; everything else has a faster lane.

steps.title: The rule nobody memorizes twice
steps.body: The quotient rule is secretly the product rule in disguise: write $\frac{f}{g} = f \cdot g^{-1}$ and run product + chain (next lesson), and the quotient formula falls out. Professional mathematicians routinely re-derive it this way rather than memorize the minus. After the chain rule, you'll be able to pull the same trick.

steps.title: What sticks
points: (f/g)′ = (f′g − fg′)/g² — low d-high minus high d-low, over low-low.
The minus makes order fatal; test on x²/x = x when unsure.
g² > 0: the derivative's sign lives in the numerator alone.

lessons.title: The Chain Rule
lessons.concreteComparison: Gears in a chain: the pedals turn the crank, the crank turns the wheel. If the crank spins 3× per pedal stroke and the wheel 5× per crank turn, the wheel spins 15× per pedal stroke — rates through a chain MULTIPLY. That's the chain rule, whole.
lessons.objective: Differentiate composite functions with the chain rule: outer derivative evaluated at the inner, times the inner derivative.
concept: **Chain rule:** for a composition $f(g(x))$ — a function INSIDE another — the derivative is $f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$: the outer function's derivative, evaluated at the UNTOUCHED inner, times the inner's derivative. In Leibniz form: $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx}$ — rates through a chain multiply.

The working recipe: spot the layers (what would you compute LAST? that's the outer), differentiate the outer leaving the inside alone, then MULTIPLY by the inside's derivative. $(x^2 + 4)^3$: outer = cube, inner $= x^2 + 4$: $3(x^2+4)^2 \cdot 2x = 6x(x^2+4)^2$.

The chain rule turns your four library derivatives into infinite families: $\sin(3x) \to 3\cos(3x)$, $e^{2x} \to 2e^{2x}$, $\ln(x^2 + 1) \to \frac{2x}{x^2+1}$, $\sqrt{x^2 + 5} \to \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+5}}$. Every 'function of a function' — which is nearly every function in applications — routes through it.
lessons.keyIdea: For f(g(x)): differentiate the outer at the untouched inner, then multiply by the inner's derivative — rates in a chain multiply.

facts.title: Chain rule
facts.explanation: Outer′ at the inner, TIMES inner′.

facts.title: Leibniz form
facts.explanation: Gears: rates through a chain multiply.

facts.title: Power shortcut
facts.explanation: The power rule with a tail — the most-used special case.

workedExamples.statement: Differentiate $y = (x^2 + 4)^3$ two ways: chain rule, and by checking the pattern makes sense against expansion.
workedExamples.solution: Layers: outer = $u^3$, inner $u = x^2 + 4$. Chain: $3(x^2 + 4)^2 \cdot 2x = 6x(x^2 + 4)^2$. Sanity: expanding $(x^2+4)^3$ starts $x^6 + 12x^4 + \ldots$, whose derivative starts $6x^5 + 48x^3 + \ldots$ — and $6x(x^2+4)^2 = 6x^5 + 48x^3 + 96x$ agrees. The chain rule just skipped the mess.

Three library functions, each with a passenger. $\sin(3x)$: $\cos(3x) \cdot 3 = 3\cos(3x)$. $e^{2x}$: $e^{2x} \cdot 2 = 2e^{2x}$. $\ln(x^2+1)$: $\frac{1}{x^2+1} \cdot 2x = \frac{2x}{x^2+1}$. The pattern never varies: library derivative at the untouched inside, times the inside's derivative.

commonMistakes.correction: That's the outer derivative WITHOUT the inner's. The chain rule is a product: $3(x^2+4)^2 \cdot 2x$. Habit: every time you differentiate an outer layer, immediately ask 'times the derivative of the inside — what is it?' Even when it's 1, ask.

commonMistakes.text: Differentiating the inside while taking the outer derivative: sin(3x) → cos(3).
commonMistakes.correction: The inner rides UNTOUCHED through the outer's derivative: $\cos(3x)$, keeping its full $3x$. The inner's derivative arrives separately, as a multiplier: $3\cos(3x)$. Two separate acts — never merge them.

tryIt.statement: Differentiate $y = \cos(x^3)$ and evaluate the slope at $x = 0$.
tryIt.solution: Outer cosine, inner $x^3$: $-\sin(x^3) \cdot 3x^2 = -3x^2 \sin(x^3)$. At 0: $-3 \cdot 0 \cdot \sin 0 = 0$ — flat at the origin.

steps.title: Functions inside functions
steps.body: $(x^2+4)^3$, $\sin(3x)$, $e^{2x}$, $\sqrt{x^2+5}$ — none of these are sums, products, or quotients. They're COMPOSITIONS: one function fed into another. Almost every function in real applications has this nested shape, which makes the chain rule the single most-used rule in calculus. Master it and the whole differentiable world opens.

steps.title: Rates in a chain multiply
steps.body: Bike gears: pedals drive the crank at 3 turns per stroke; the crank drives the wheel at 5 turns per turn. Wheel per stroke? $3 \times 5 = 15$ — rates through a chain MULTIPLY. Leibniz notation says it perfectly: $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx}$. In function form: $[f(g(x))]' = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$ — outer′ at the untouched inner, times inner′.

steps.title: Name the layers
steps.prompt: In $y = (x^2 + 4)^3$, what are the outer and inner functions?
options: There are no layers — it's a polynomial
steps.explanation: Ask 'what would I compute LAST?' Given a number, you'd square-and-add-4 first, cube last — so the cube is the OUTER layer. Layer-spotting is 90% of every chain-rule problem.

steps.title: Never forget the tail
steps.body: The #1 chain-rule error is amputation: writing $3(x^2+4)^2$ and stopping. The inner function changes too, and its rate MULTIPLIES on: $\cdot \, 2x$. Build the reflex: outer derivative first, then immediately — 'times the derivative of the inside.' Say it every time, even when the inside is just $x$ and the multiplier is 1.

steps.title: Catch the amputation
steps.explanation: Outer $e^u$ keeps its shape at the untouched inner ($e^{5x}$), times the inner's derivative ($5$): $5e^{5x}$. Option B forgot the tail; option C tried the power rule on an exponential — Unit 2's forbidden move.

steps.title: Work outside-in, one layer per line
steps.body: For deep nests like $\sqrt{\sin(x^2)}$, peel one layer per line: $\frac{1}{2\sqrt{\sin(x^2)}}$ (outer), $\cdot \cos(x^2)$ (middle), $\cdot 2x$ (inner). Three layers, three factors, multiplied. Depth never adds difficulty — only length.

steps.title: What sticks
points: [f(g(x))]′ = f′(g(x)) · g′(x): outer′ at the UNTOUCHED inner, times inner′.
Spot layers by 'what do I compute last?'; that's the outer.
The tail is not optional — every layer contributes one multiplied factor.

lessons.title: Combining Rules & Second Derivatives
lessons.concreteComparison: A chef who's mastered knife skills, heat, and seasoning doesn't use them one at a time — a single dish needs all three, in the right order. Real derivatives are the same: one function can demand product, chain, AND quotient in a single computation. The skill now is orchestration.
lessons.objective: Differentiate functions that need several rules at once, and compute second derivatives — the derivative of the derivative.
concept: Mixed problems are parsed from the OUTERMOST structure in: $x^2(x+1)^3$ is a PRODUCT (whose second factor needs the chain rule); $\frac{\sin x}{x^2+1}$ is a QUOTIENT (whose top is a library function). Name the top-level structure first, apply its rule, and let the inner rules fire as they're needed.

Differentiate the derivative and you get the **second derivative** $f''$ — the rate of change OF the rate of change. Notation: $f''(x)$ or $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$. If $f$ is position, $f'$ is velocity and $f''$ is acceleration — how fast the speed itself is changing. Nothing new mechanically: run your rules on $f'$.

$f''$ has its own graphical voice — it measures how the SLOPE is trending. $f'' > 0$: slopes are increasing, the curve bends upward (cups up); $f'' < 0$: it bends down. Unit 4 builds the peak/valley machinery on exactly this reading.
lessons.keyIdea: Parse from the outermost structure in — product of a chain, quotient of a library function — and iterate: f″ is just the rules run on f′, reading acceleration and bend.

facts.title: Parse outside-in
facts.explanation: Name the top-level structure, then let inner rules fire.

facts.title: Second derivative
facts.explanation: The rules, run again on f′.

facts.title: The kinematics ladder
facts.explanation: Position, velocity, acceleration — one derivative per rung.

workedExamples.solution: Top level: a PRODUCT. Turns: $2x \cdot (x+1)^3 + x^2 \cdot [(x+1)^3]'$. The second turn needs the chain rule: $3(x+1)^2 \cdot 1$. Assemble: $2x(x+1)^3 + 3x^2(x+1)^2$ — factorable to $x(x+1)^2(5x + 2)$, handy for finding the flat points.

workedExamples.statement: A particle's position is $s(t) = t^3 - 3t^2 + 2t$ meters after $t$ seconds. Find its velocity and acceleration functions, and the acceleration at $t = 2$.
workedExamples.solution: One rung per derivative: $v(t) = s'(t) = 3t^2 - 6t + 2$; $a(t) = v'(t) = 6t - 6$. At $t = 2$: $a(2) = 6$ m/s² — speeding up, at an increasing clip.

commonMistakes.text: Starting with an inner rule because it's the one you see first.
commonMistakes.correction: Structure is decided at the TOP: $\frac{\sin x}{x^2+1}$ is a quotient (of nice pieces), not 'a sine problem.' Parse outermost-in — name the top-level structure out loud before differentiating anything.

commonMistakes.text: Treating f″ as (f′)² or as f′ evaluated twice.
commonMistakes.correction: $f''$ means DIFFERENTIATE AGAIN: run the rules on $f'$. For $f = x^4$: $f' = 4x^3$, $f'' = 12x^2$ — not $(4x^3)^2$. The prime is an operation, not a factor.

tryIt.solution: Quotient with a library top: $\frac{\cos x (x^2 + 1) - \sin x \cdot 2x}{(x^2 + 1)^2}$. Two rules, one line — the quotient shape leads, the wave derivative fires inside.

tryIt.statement: Find $f''(x)$ for $f(x) = x^4 - 3x^2 + \sin x$, and evaluate $f''(0)$.
tryIt.solution: First: $f' = 4x^3 - 6x + \cos x$. Again: $f'' = 12x^2 - 6 - \sin x$. At 0: $-6 - 0 = -6$ — the curve bends downward through $x = 0$.

steps.title: Rules don't come one per problem
steps.body: You now own the full toolkit: power, library, product, quotient, chain. Real functions use several at once — $x^2(x+1)^3$ is a product hiding a chain; $\frac{\sin x}{x^2+1}$ is a quotient carrying a wave. The skill that separates clean answers from tangles: **parse from the outermost structure in**. Name the top-level shape FIRST; inner rules fire on demand.

steps.title: Name the top level
steps.prompt: To differentiate $y = \dfrac{(x^2 + 1)^4}{x}$, which rule applies FIRST (outermost)?
options: Quotient rule — it's a fraction of two expressions
Chain rule — there's a power of a sum
Product rule — two things are multiplied inside
steps.explanation: The LAST thing you'd compute by hand is the division — so the quotient shape governs. The chain rule fires inside it, when the quotient rule asks for the top's derivative $4(x^2+1)^3 \cdot 2x$.

steps.title: The derivative of the derivative
steps.body: Since $f'$ is itself a function, nothing stops you differentiating AGAIN: $f'' = (f')'$, the **second derivative** ($\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ in Leibniz dress). It measures how the SLOPE is trending: $f'' > 0$ means slopes are climbing — the curve cups upward; $f'' < 0$ means it bends down. No new mechanics; same rules, second pass.

steps.title: The kinematics ladder
steps.body: The famous application: position $s(t)$, one derivative gives velocity $v = s'$, another gives ACCELERATION $a = s''$ — how fast the speed itself changes. When you feel a car press you into the seat, you're feeling a second derivative. Physics writes its deepest laws ($F = ma$) at the second-derivative level, which is precisely why calculus was invented.

steps.title: Climb the ladder
steps.explanation: Two passes of the power rule: $f' = 5x^4$, then $f'' = 20x^3$. Option C squared the first derivative — but the prime is an operation to repeat, never a factor to multiply.

steps.title: The jerk is real
steps.body: Keep differentiating position and the names keep coming: the THIRD derivative is called jerk — the lurch you feel when a driver stomps the brake unevenly. Engineers designing elevators and roller coasters limit jerk explicitly. (Fourth, fifth, sixth have whimsical names too: snap, crackle, pop.)

steps.title: What sticks
points: Parse outside-in: name the top-level structure, let inner rules fire on demand.
f″ = rules run on f′ — slope's trend, the curve's bend, physics' acceleration.
Position → velocity → acceleration: one derivative per rung.

practice.statement: Differentiate $y = (x^2 - 1)(x^3 + 2x)$ with the product rule, then verify by expanding.
Differentiate $y = x^2 e^x$ and find where the tangent is horizontal.
practice.solution: $y' = 2x e^x + x^2 e^x = x e^x (2 + x)$. Since $e^x > 0$ always: horizontal at $x = 0$ and $x = -2$.

practice.statement: Differentiate $y = \sqrt{4x^2 + 9}$ and evaluate the slope at $x = 2$.
Differentiate $y = e^{3x} \sin x$ (product of two chains).
practice.solution: Product: $3e^{3x}\sin x + e^{3x}\cos x = e^{3x}(3\sin x + \cos x)$.

practice.statement: For $s(t) = t^3 - 9t^2 + 24t$ (meters, seconds): find $v(t)$ and $a(t)$, and the times when the particle is momentarily stopped.

testYourself.statement: Differentiate $y = x^3(2x - 1)^2$ and factor your answer.
testYourself.solution: Product + chain: $3x^2(2x-1)^2 + x^3 \cdot 2(2x-1) \cdot 2 = x^2(2x-1)(10x - 3)$ after factoring out $x^2(2x-1)$.
Chain twice: $\frac{6x}{3x^2 + 1}$, and $e^{-x^2} \cdot (-2x) = -2x e^{-x^2}$ — the bell curve's derivative.

testYourself.statement: Find $f''(x)$ for $f(x) = (x^2 + 1)^3$ — chain rule, then product-with-chain on the second pass.
Differentiate $y = \sin^2 x$ (that is, $(\sin x)^2$) and simplify using a double-angle identity.
testYourself.solution: Power-with-tail: $2\sin x \cdot \cos x$ — which the identity compresses to $\sin(2x)$. Chain rule and trig identities meeting mid-page.

testYourself.statement: A ball's height is $h(t) = 30t - 5t^2$ meters after $t$ seconds. Find the velocity and acceleration functions, the moment the ball is momentarily still, and interpret $h''$.
testYourself.solution: $v(t) = 30 - 10t$: still when $30 - 10t = 0$, at $t = 3$ s (the peak). $a(t) = h''(t) = -10$ m/s² at every instant — constant downward pull; that's gravity (rounded), showing up as a second derivative.
